infobox.title: Fraud Detection System
infobox.imageCaption: Admin dashboard showing ML prediction analytics

lead:
The **Online Transaction Fraud Detection System** is my MCA Final Year Academic Project (3rd Semester) — a full-stack banking fraud detection platform I built using **ASP.NET Web Forms** and a **4-algorithm ML ensemble**. Every transaction is classified by Logistic Regression, KNN, SVM, and a Convolutional Neural Network — if 3 or more algorithms vote fraudulent, the transaction is automatically blocked.

The system includes real-time fraud detection, an intrusion detection module that tracks failed logins by IP and geolocation, SMTP email alerts, and a full admin panel with Google Charts analytics. The code is publicly on [GitHub](https://github.com/lingadevaru-hp/online-fraud-detection-system).

content.content:
I implemented all four ML algorithms directly in C# rather than using a Python ML library. This was a deliberate constraint I set for myself: the entire stack had to be a single ASP.NET application, not a Python microservice. It forced me to understand the mathematics of each algorithm rather than just calling sklearn.

My **ensemble decision rule** is simple and interpretable: majority voting. If 3 or more of the 4 algorithms independently classify a transaction as fraudulent, the transaction is blocked. This reduces false positives compared to single-algorithm approaches while maintaining sensitivity.

rows.How I used it: Binary fraud/no-fraud classification on 4 normalised features
K=3 nearest neighbours in feature space
Support Vector Machine — finds maximum-margin decision boundary
Convolutional Neural Network — the only deep learning model in the ensemble

content.title: Ensemble decision rule
content.content: 3 or more out of 4 algorithms vote fraud → transaction blocked. This majority-voting approach is more robust than any single algorithm and reduces both false positives and false negatives compared to relying on the CNN alone.

rows.Detail: Value of the transaction in rupees
Frequency of recent transactions from the account
0 = known safe location, 1 = flagged risky location
0 = business hours, 1 = odd hours (late night / early morning)

content.content:
**Fraud Detection:** Real-time ensemble ML voting on every transaction. Transaction blocked immediately when 3+ algorithms vote fraud — no human review queue.

**Intrusion Detection:** I track failed logins with IP address, GPS geolocation, and timestamp. Auto account blocking triggers when 5+ failed transactions occur within 1 hour. SMTP email alerts are sent on suspicious activity.

**Admin Panel:** Full user management, bank CRUD operations, intrusion log viewer, transaction analytics with Google Charts, and an ML prediction dashboard showing per-algorithm vote counts.

**User Side:** Supports creating bank accounts (SBI, HDFC, Karnataka Bank), adding wallet funds, transferring money with live fraud detection, viewing account statements, and a per-account security key required for all transactions.

content.title: Security measures I implemented
content.content: Parameterized SQL queries throughout (SQL injection prevention). Session management with proper logout. Security key per bank account required for all transaction operations.

sections.title: Why I Chose C# Over Python

content.content:
My 3rd semester final year academic submission for the MCA at SIT Tumakuru. The 4th (final) semester is reserved for industry internship — this project was the 3rd semester deliverable.

I deliberately chose C# and ASP.NET rather than Python and Flask. C# and .NET represent a professional enterprise stack, and implementing ML algorithms from scratch in a statically-typed language forced me to understand the underlying mathematics rather than just calling a Python library. It's a harder route that taught me more.

For contrast with my CHIAC ASI internship ML work (Python, QLoRA, transformer models), see [[thoshan-flash|Thoshan Flash]].
